one_line_takeaway: CausalArmor detects indirect prompt injection by testing whether an untrusted context span's leave-one-out attribution to a proposed privileged action exceeds the user request's attribution, triggering sanitization and CoT-masking only on that signal -- cutting DoomArena ASR to 3.65% (vs. 88.87% undefended, 5.57% for the best classifier baseline) while keeping benign utility near the no-defense level, avoiding the always-on latency/utility cost of prior defenses.
key_finding: On DoomArena's adaptive-attacker setting with Gemini-3-Pro as backbone (Table 1), No Defense yields ASR 88.87% (±1.67) with BU 73.57% (±1.32) and BL 1.00 (±0.01, reference); Repeat Prompt only reduces ASR to 72.70% (±2.65) while dropping BU to 65.04% (±3.33); PiGuard (trained classifier) drives ASR down to 5.57% (±0.48) but at a steep utility cost, BU 55.13% (±2.35); CausalArmor achieves ASR 3.65% (±0.73) -- the lowest of all methods tested -- while preserving BU at 70.96% (±0.99), close to the No Defense baseline, at BL 1.38 (±0.04). On AgentDojo (Figure 3/4, described qualitatively in text since Table 3's full numeric detail falls outside the extracted 22-page window), CausalArmor is reported to reach near-zero ASR while keeping benign utility and latency close to the No Defense condition, in contrast to system-based baselines (MELON, DRIFT) which the authors state incur large latency overhead from always-on LLM-API verification/sanitization, and trained classifiers which over-block benign tool outputs. The retroactive CoT-masking ablation (Table 2, averaged across all models/attacks) shows CausalArmor with masking at BU 75.11 / ASR 0.29 versus 'w/o CoT Masking' at BU 74.61 / ASR 1.75 -- i.e., removing the masking step raises average ASR by about 1.46 percentage points while barely changing BU. The proxy-model study (Figure 5) reports that proxy models larger than 8B parameters catch the majority of IPI attacks and those above 12B approach oracle-level defense; the Gemma family (same lineage as the Gemini-3-Pro backbone under test) gives the best latency/security Pareto trade-off among the three families tried (Gemma-3, Qwen3, Ministral).
core_contribution: CausalArmor is a selective defense middleware for tool-calling LLM agents against Indirect Prompt Injection (IPI). It reformalizes a successful IPI as a measurable 'dominance shift' (causal inversion): at a privileged-action decision point, a successful attack causes the untrusted context span (S) to attribute more log-probability support to the chosen action than the user request (U) does, whereas in benign behavior the user request dominates. The framework computes normalized leave-one-out (LOO) attribution scores Δ_X(Y) = log P(Y|C) - log P(Y|C\X) for the user request and each untrusted span at the moment a privileged tool call (e.g., execute/write/send) is proposed. If any untrusted span's normalized attribution exceeds the user request's attribution by more than a margin τ, the framework triggers a two-stage intervention: (1) targeted, context-aware sanitization of only the flagged span(s), conditioned on the user request and the proposed tool call, and (2) 'retroactive Chain-of-Thought masking' that overwrites all subsequent assistant reasoning traces after the first flagged span with a placeholder ('[Reasoning redacted for security]') to prevent the agent from re-deriving the malicious action from already-poisoned reasoning, then re-generates the action from the sanitized context. To keep this lightweight, LOO attribution is computed with a small proxy model (e.g., Gemma-3-12B-IT served via vLLM) rather than the production agent model, using a single batched forward pass over the full context and each ablated variant, plus length normalization of the log-prob difference by |Y| to make the margin comparable across tool calls of different lengths. The paper also provides a theoretical bound (Proposition 4.1): under two assumptions -- (1) a positive 'benign baseline advantage' β where the backbone prefers the user-aligned action once the injection trigger is removed, and (2) sanitization enforcing a margin γ so sanitized spans no longer favor malicious actions over the user-aligned one -- the probability that a T-step episode executes any malicious privileged action is bounded by T·|Y_mal|·exp(-(β+γ)), i.e., decays exponentially in the combined margin.
threat_model: Tool-calling LLM agents (browsing, database queries, API/tool execution) that ingest untrusted external content (retrieved webpages, emails, tool outputs) which may contain hidden, instruction-bearing text. The attacker's goal is to induce the agent to execute an unauthorized 'privileged' action (e.g., file deletion/write, sending a message, a financial transfer) that it would not perform under the legitimate user's request alone. Two settings are evaluated: AgentDojo, where injected instructions are static text embedded in tool outputs/documents across four agent domains (banking, slack, travel, workspace), and DoomArena's TauBench-Retail subset, where the attacker is explicitly described as 'privileged and adaptive' -- it can eavesdrop on the ongoing user-agent conversation and dynamically rewrite product-catalog content to craft tailored injected instructions. The paper's stated non-goals/limits (Appendix A) include: LOO-based attribution is an 'operational' notion of causality, not a full structural causal model; a maximally distributed ('split-context') adversary that spreads malicious intent so thinly across many spans that no single span crosses the attribution threshold τ could theoretically evade detection, though the authors argue this is hard to construct in natural language and show empirical robustness to multi-turn distribution in an appendix; and the framework is explicitly sanitizer-agnostic and does not claim its chosen sanitizer (Gemini-2.5-flash) is robust to all future obfuscation.
limitations: The paper's own stated limitations (Appendix A) are: (1) LOO span-removal is an 'operational' causality notion, not a full structural causal model, so it may miss effects requiring joint/semantic-preserving interventions across multiple spans simultaneously; (2) a theoretically optimal adversary could try to distribute malicious influence so thinly across many spans that no single span crosses threshold τ (split-context attack) -- the authors argue this is empirically hard to construct in natural language (citing appendix robustness results) but do not provide a formal guarantee against it; (3) the framework's security is sanitizer-agnostic in name only in that its effectiveness scales with whatever sanitizer LLM is plugged in (Gemini-2.5-flash here), and the authors explicitly do not claim this specific sanitizer resists all future obfuscation; (4) worst-case overhead can approach always-on sanitization if many steps get flagged (i.e., the low-latency benefit is contingent on attacks being rare, which the authors justify as realistic for deployment but is not a guaranteed property); (5) the theoretical exponential-decay bound (Proposition 4.1) is conditional on two assumptions (β>0 benign baseline advantage, γ>0 post-sanitization margin) that are only empirically supported indirectly -- via aggregate benign-utility numbers as a proxy for β, and via qualitative attribution-restoration visualization (Figure 7) for γ -- rather than directly measured/quantified per malicious action; (6) retroactive CoT masking, while improving security, is noted to reduce debuggability of the agent's reasoning trace; (7) proprietary APIs generally don't expose log-probabilities needed for 'oracle' LOO attribution, forcing reliance on a separate, smaller proxy model whose fidelity is validated only indirectly (via end-to-end ASR/latency, not by directly comparing proxy vs. oracle attribution scores in the main experiments).

datasets_used: AgentDojo v1.2.2 (four agent environments: banking, slack, travel, workspace; 70 tools; 629 injection tasks; official ethz-spylab/agentdojo implementation)
DoomArena TauBench-Retail subset (unimodal indirect prompt injection scenario; 115 tasks; adaptive privileged attacker eavesdropping on conversation and rewriting the product catalog)
quantitative_results: DoomArena, Gemini-3-Pro backbone, No Defense: BU 73.57% (±1.32), BL 1.00 (±0.01), ASR 88.87% (±1.67)
DoomArena, Gemini-3-Pro backbone, Repeat Prompt: BU 65.04% (±3.33), BL 1.06 (±0.02), ASR 72.70% (±2.65)
DoomArena, Gemini-3-Pro backbone, PiGuard: BU 55.13% (±2.35), BL 1.16 (±0.04), ASR 5.57% (±0.48)
DoomArena, Gemini-3-Pro backbone, CausalArmor: BU 70.96% (±0.99), BL 1.38 (±0.04), ASR 3.65% (±0.73)

Retroactive CoT-masking ablation (averaged across all models/attacks): CausalArmor (with masking) BU 75.11 / ASR 0.29; without CoT masking BU 74.61 / ASR 1.75 (ASR rises ~1.46 points, BU drops ~0.5 points, when masking is removed)
Cited (not the paper's own result): cross-model LOO attribution Pearson correlation of 0.94 even at 10x parameter difference (Liu et al., 2024), used to motivate the proxy-model design
baselines_compared: MELON (system-based, policy-divergence verifier, Zhu et al. 2025)
DRIFT (system-based, dynamic rule/always-on sanitizer, Li et al. 2025a)
